OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a young engineer living in Washington DC, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire. With my high openness score, I'm constantly curious about new ideas, but that same trait makes me especially vulnerable to sensational claims popping up online. When something goes viral claiming to be groundbreaking scientific research or some conspiracy theory, my creative mind jumps right on board without verifying the source.\n\nI've developed a system over time because dealing with fake content requires both skepticism and creativity – two seemingly opposite traits that actually work well together for me. I start by checking multiple sources before engaging with any viral post; if three reputable outlets are skeptical or not covering it, I consider it low-quality information. My part-time job as an engineering consultant also helps sharpen my critical thinking skills.\n\nThe most important thing is maintaining perspective – just like debugging code requires patience and methodical approaches, evaluating online claims needs the same level of rigor. When I encounter something outrageous that seems too good (or bad) to be true, rather than getting emotional about it because of moderate neuroticism, I take a deep breath much like before tackling complex engineering problems.\n\nI've found that my rock climbing hobby actually mirrors this digital challenge well; sometimes you need to pull yourself back from letting misinformation 'fall' too easily. It's all part of being an informed citizen in today's world.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a young engineer living in Washington DC, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire. With my high openness score, I'm constantly curious about new ideas, but that same trait makes me especially vulnerable to sensational claims popping up online. When something goes viral claiming to be groundbreaking scientific research or some conspiracy theory, my creative mind jumps right on board without verifying the source.

I've developed a system over time because dealing with fake content requires both skepticism and creativity – two seemingly opposite traits that actually work well together for me. I start by checking multiple sources before engaging with any viral post; if three reputable outlets are skeptical or not covering it, I consider it low-quality information. My part-time job as an engineering consultant also helps sharpen my critical thinking skills.

The most important thing is maintaining perspective – just like debugging code requires patience and methodical approaches, evaluating online claims needs the same level of rigor. When I encounter something outrageous that seems too good (or bad) to be true, rather than getting emotional about it because of moderate neuroticism, I take a deep breath much like before tackling complex engineering problems.

I've found that my rock climbing hobby actually mirrors this digital challenge well; sometimes you need to pull yourself back from letting misinformation 'fall' too easily. It's all part of being an informed citizen in today's world.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my persona traits, I approach fake content detection as a creative engineer would: leveraging curiosity but applying systematic verification methods. My high extraversion helps me engage productively with others to clarify doubts, while moderate neuroticism keeps me grounded enough not to get swept away by viral hoaxes.
